event: Austronesian-speaking Lapita peoples voyaged into the archipelago, becoming the first humans to settle the islands of Vanuatu.
Lapita potters established coastal communities across the islands, leaving distinctive dentate-stamped ceramics found at sites such as Teouma on Efate.
The Teouma cemetery on Efate, one of the oldest Lapita burial grounds in the Pacific, was in active use by the early settlers.
Lapita pottery traditions faded as locally distinct Melanesian cultures, languages, and chiefly societies developed across the islands.
Oral tradition holds that the powerful chief Roy Mata united communities across central Vanuatu before his death and elaborate burial on Retoka Island.
The volcanic eruption of Kuwae devastated the central islands, splitting an island into Epi and Tongoa and reshaping local societies.
Portuguese navigator Pedro Fernandes de Queiros, sailing for Spain, became the first European to reach the islands, landing on Espiritu Santo.
French explorer Louis-Antoine de Bougainville sailed through the islands, proving they were not part of a southern continent.
British captain James Cook charted the archipelago and named it the New Hebrides after the Scottish islands.
The discovery of sandalwood spurred a wave of European and Australian traders into the islands, disrupting local communities.
Missionary John Williams of the London Missionary Society was killed and eaten on Erromango, marking an early and violent phase of Christian evangelization.
Presbyterian missionary John Geddie arrived on Aneityum, beginning sustained and ultimately influential Christian conversion in the south.
The labour trade known as blackbirding intensified, with thousands of islanders taken to work on plantations in Queensland and Fiji.
Britain and France established a Joint Naval Commission to protect their nationals amid rising rivalry over the islands.
Britain and France formalized joint rule by creating the Anglo-French Condominium of the New Hebrides, a unique dual colonial government.
A revised Condominium protocol entrenched parallel British and French administrations, courts, schools, and currencies on the islands.
Allied forces established a massive military base on Espiritu Santo, making it a key staging ground for the Pacific campaign of World War II.
Departing American forces dumped vast quantities of surplus equipment into the sea at Million Dollar Point on Espiritu Santo.
The wartime experience of American wealth fed the John Frum cargo cult on Tanna, which awaited the return of material goods.
Father Walter Lini and others founded the New Hebrides Cultural Association, which grew into the nationalist Vanua'aku Party.
Negotiations between the colonial powers and local leaders set a timetable toward self-government and independence.
The Vanua'aku Party won elections and Walter Lini became Chief Minister as the colony moved toward full independence.
Vanuatu gained independence on 30 July, with Walter Lini as its first Prime Minister, after suppressing the Santo secessionist Coconut War.
Vanuatu was admitted to the United Nations as a member state.
Cyclone Uma struck the islands, causing widespread destruction and dozens of deaths.
Founding Prime Minister Walter Lini was ousted from leadership, ending more than a decade of Vanua'aku Party dominance.
Vanuatu joined the World Trade Organization process and deepened its role within the Pacific Islands Forum and regional institutions.
A powerful earthquake struck near Port Vila, damaging buildings in the capital.
Vanuatu was ranked the happiest nation on Earth by the Happy Planet Index, drawing global attention to its way of life.
Vanuatu formally acceded to the World Trade Organization as a full member.
Cyclone Pam, one of the most intense storms ever recorded in the Pacific, devastated Vanuatu and affected most of the population.
The volcanic island of Ambae was evacuated as the Manaro volcano erupted, displacing the entire population.
Category 5 Cyclone Harold struck during the COVID-19 pandemic, compounding hardship and complicating relief efforts.
Vanuatu led a successful campaign at the United Nations General Assembly seeking an International Court of Justice advisory opinion on climate change obligations.
A major earthquake struck Port Vila in December, killing more than a dozen people and damaging the capital's infrastructure.
